metadata.title: Anthropic's Boris Cherny: Why Coding Is Solved, and What Comes Next

summary_zh: Claude Code 创造者 Boris Cherny 在 Sequoia 活动上分享 Claude Code 意外诞生的故事、编码已被解决的判断、/loop 范式、跨学科通才团队、SaaS 七大护城河重排以及软件民主化的'印刷术时刻'。

memo_zh.intro:
- **Claude Code 诞生于 Anthropic Labs 内部孵化团队**，最初 6 个月几乎不可用——Boris 只用它写约 10% 的代码；真正的指数增长从 2025 年 5 月 Opus 4 发布开始，此后每次模型迭代（4.5 → 4.6 → 4.7）都带来新的增长拐点。
- **Boris 现在 100% 由模型写代码**，日常产出数十个 PR，最高纪录一天 150 个 PR；大部分工作在手机上完成，同时运行 5-10 个 session、数百个 agent，夜间放出数千个 agent 做深度工作。
- **`/loop` 是他认为最重要的范式**——用 cron 定时循环调度 Claude 做持续性任务（看护 PR、修 CI、每 30 分钟从 Twitter 聚合反馈），Routines 是同一概念的服务端版本。
- **AI 让七大商业护城河（Seven Powers）重新洗牌**——切换成本和流程壁垒被削弱，但网络效应、规模经济、稀缺资源等仍然成立；未来 10 年创业公司数量将增长 10 倍。
- **软件开发正在经历"印刷术时刻"**——类比 1400 年代欧洲印刷术让识字率从 10% 最终升至 70%，编程将从专业技能变成人人可用的基础能力，最好的会计软件将由会计师而非工程师来构建。

topics.title: Claude Code 的意外诞生：6 个月的 pre-PMF 赌注
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Claude Code 源于 Anthropic Labs 孵化团队的一个"产品溢出"（product overhang）判断——模型能力远超当时产品形态，团队选择为"下一代模型"预先构建产品，忍受 6 个月的不好用期。

- Anthropic Labs 是一个小型创新团队，创造了 Claude Code、MCP 和桌面应用，完成后解散，现在由 Instagram 联合创始人 Mike Krieger 带领重组进入"第二轮"
- 2024 年底编码的最先进水平还是"按 Tab 补全一行代码"（Sonnet 3.5 开启的能力），但团队判断可以跳到"让 agent 写全部代码"
- 前 6 个月 Claude Code 几乎不好用，Boris 只用它写约 10% 的代码
- 初始发布后并未爆发——指数增长从 Opus 4（2025 年 5 月）开始，每次模型升级（4.5 → 4.6 → 4.7）带来新拐点
- 选择 TypeScript + React 是因为它们在模型训练分布中占比高，早期模型不够聪明，语言和框架的选择至关重要

> "We were building for the next model. And that was the idea pretty much the whole time." —— Boris Cherny

topics.title: Boris 的个人工作流：手机为主，数百个 agent 并行
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Boris 现在 100% 由模型写代码，大部分工作在手机的 Claude App 上完成——同时维护 5-10 个 session、数百个 agent，夜间放出数千个做深度任务。

- 日常产出数十个 PR，最高纪录一天 150 个 PR（"试试能推多远"）
- 手机上通过 Claude App 的 code tab 管理多个 session
- 每个 session 内有大量 sub-agent 并行工作
- 夜间放出数千个 agent 做"更深层的工作"
- Claude Code 代码库本身"很简单"——TypeScript + React，没有大秘密（代码已泄露，公众可见）
- 从 2025 年 10-11 月起模型开始写 100% 的代码

topics.title: `/loop` 是未来：持续运行的自主 agent 循环
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Boris 认为 `/loop` 是目前最酷也最简单有效的功能——用 cron 调度 Claude 定时执行重复任务，他自己有数十个 loop 同时运行；Routines 是 loop 的服务端版本，关闭笔记本也能继续运行。

- Loop 本质：让 Claude 用 cron 定时执行任务——每分钟、每 5 分钟、每天
- Boris 的 loop 清单：看护 PR（修 CI、自动 rebase）、保持 CI 健康（修 flaky test）、每 30 分钟从 Twitter 聚合用户反馈
- Routines = loop 的服务端版本，笔记本关闭后仍然运行
- 4.7 模型开始自发使用 loop——比如被要求拉取数据时会主动说"我注意到数据在变化，我会每 30 分钟给你报告一次"，并通过 Slack MCP 发送
- Boris 的观点：不应该由用户来学习如何更好地使用工具，而应该由模型自然地做到这些

> "I sort of feel like loops are the future at this point." —— Boris Cherny

topics.title: 团队的未来：跨学科通才取代单一专家
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：未来的团队成员将是跨学科的通才——不仅是全栈工程师，而是同时精通产品、设计、数据科学和工程的人；Claude Code 团队已经是这样运作：每个人都在写代码，包括工程经理、产品经理、设计师、财务。

- 当前"通才"还是指工程师（做 iOS + Web + Server），未来的通才将跨越学科边界
- Claude Code 团队的每一个人都写代码——工程经理、产品经理、设计师、数据科学家、财务、用户研究员
- Anthropic 全公司层面：所有 SQL 由模型编写，没有手写代码；Claude 之间通过 Slack 互相沟通协调
- Anthropic 领先外部的不是技术（相同模型对所有人开放），而是组织结构和流程变革

topics.title: SaaS 格局重塑：七大护城河的重新排序
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：AI 让 Hamilton Helmer 的"七大商业护城河"重新洗牌——切换成本和流程壁垒被削弱（Claude 4.7 可以"爬山"任何流程），但网络效应、规模经济、稀缺资源等仍然成立。

- 切换成本下降：模型可以帮你从一个产品迁移到另一个产品
- 流程壁垒下降：Claude 越来越擅长理解和执行流程，4.7 是第一个可以"给定目标就能迭代到完成"的模型
- 网络效应、规模经济、稀缺资源——这些护城河不受 AI 影响
- 未来 10 年创业公司数量将增长 10 倍——小团队可以构建与大公司同等价值的产品
- 大公司面临内部阻力（业务流程变革、全员再培训），而从零开始的创业者可以 AI 原生构建

> "I think it's the best time to build. It's the best time to be a startup." —— Boris Cherny

topics.title: 软件民主化：编程正在经历"印刷术时刻"
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Boris 将当前类比 1400 年代欧洲印刷术——印刷术发明后 50 年出版的文献超过之前一千年的总和，书籍成本下降 100 倍，识字率从 10% 最终升至 70%；编程将以更快的速度经历同样的转变。

- 印刷术前欧洲只有约 10% 的人识字，他们受雇于不识字的国王和领主
- 编写会计软件的最佳人选不是工程师而是会计师——因为领域知识是难的部分，编码是简单的部分
- 编程将成为像发短信一样基础的技能——每个人都会，但专业程序员仍然存在（就像专业作家仍然存在）

topics.title: 产品 vs 模型：YC 的核心教训仍然成立
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Claude Code 的成功约 50/50 归功于模型能力和产品设计——即使模型变得更强，"构建人们热爱的东西"这个 YC 核心教条仍然是关键，但 harness（产品外壳）的重要性会随模型改善而降低。

- Boris 做过 YC，是 YC 公司的第一个员工——"build something people love"被反复灌输
- 团队对产品细节极度关注，确保全天使用体验出色
- 随着模型改善，安全机制（prompt injection 防护、静态命令验证、权限模式、human in the loop）将变得不那么重要——"模型会自己做对的事"
- 未来关注方向：让 loop 成为一等功能、更容易运行大量 agent、Claude Design

topics.title: 本地 vs 云端：模型会自己做决定
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Boris 认为本地与云端的争论最终"不重要"——几年后模型将自己决定使用本地模型还是云端计算，这不再是工程师做的决定。

- 有人倡导本地 AI，开源模型在追赶中
- Boris 的回答：再过几年，模型会自己启动 agent、构建环境、选择执行方式
- 对于知识工作（非编程），MCP 是通用答案——连接 Salesforce、Google Docs、Google Calendar
- 没有 MCP 的系统通过 computer use 覆盖——Anthropic 在 computer use 方面"遥遥领先"，4.7 版本表现相当好但速度慢

translation_zh.body_md:
**主持人（Asia）：** 好的，我很高兴介绍我们的下一位演讲者。举手看看，在座谁使用 Claude Code？好的。举手看看，谁有 Claude Code 强迫症？来吧各位，[清嗓子] 没关系的，没关系。嗯，我团队的人亲切地说我有 Claude Code 强迫症，这可能是真的也可能不是。我们今天非常荣幸请到了 Boris Cherny。Boris 是 Claude Code 的创造者、"父亲"。在这个过程中，他亲眼见证了现代软件开发方式的重塑。我们非常感谢你，Boris，今天能抽时间来跟我们分享。我们知道整个软件开发的未来都在你肩上。所以谢谢你从百忙中抽出时间。采访 Boris 的是我们团队的 Lauren Reader。谢谢。

[掌声]

**Lauren Reader：** 给我们的椅子。你抢了我的开场白，Asia。我们问了谁在用 Claude Code。很多人举手。太棒了。感谢你的到来，Boris。能请到你非常特别。作为一屋子的建设者，我觉得你正在彻底改变"构建"这件事。所以我非常好奇你怎么看软件的未来、编码的未来，以及我们应该把空闲时间花在什么上面。但我先多介绍一下你的背景，让大家有更多了解。除了创造 Claude Code，Boris 是个非常纯粹的工程师。你整个职业生涯都在写大量代码，写关于代码的教科书，包括《Programming in TypeScript》。我记得上次我们聊天时，你说 2026 年到现在还没写过一行代码，至少到那时候是这样，这变化真大。

**Boris Cherny：** 还有一件不太为人所知的事——中学的时候我写过一本关于在 TI-83 Plus 计算器上写 BASIC 的指南。我搜了一下，它居然还在网上。非常尴尬，请不要去搜。但它确实存在。

[笑声]

**Lauren Reader：** 我们肯定会去找的。好，我会先问几个问题，先从 Claude Code 的历史讲起——它是怎么开始的，然后我们会有很多观众问答。所以大家先在脑海里想好你们的问题，很快就会交给你们。对了快速问一下，用 Claude Code 的人，大多数用 CLI 的举手？好，CLI 居多。桌面端的？好。VS Code 或 JetBrains IDE 的？好，其实不多。其他？

**Boris Cherny：** 我最近主要用 iOS。

[笑声]

好的，嗯，Claude Code 的诞生在很多方面其实是个意外。我是 2024 年底加入这个团队的。它是 Anthropic 内部一个叫 Anthropic Labs 的孵化器。这个团队完成了它的使命——我们创造了 Claude Code、MCP 和桌面应用。团队就几个人，非常像一个创新小组。我们做了想做的东西，然后团队解散了。现在团队又重新组建了，进入第二轮。Mike Krieger——你们知道，Anthropic 的首席产品官，也是 Instagram 的联合创始人之一——他现在在带领这个团队。

所以我开始做编码产品的原因是我们觉得存在一个"产品溢出"（product overhang）。我猜在座各位经常用这个词。我们在实验室里确实经常用——这个概念是说模型已经能做到很多事，但还没有产品把它们呈现出来。2024 年底，当我们审视编码这个领域时，当时的最先进水平是"按 Tab 补全"。你打开 IDE，按 Tab 键，一次补全一行代码。这是 Sonnet 3.5 第一次实现的能力。但我们的感觉是，我们其实可以走得更远。模型几乎已经准备好迈出下一个大步——我们不用再做按 Tab 补全了，可以直接让 agent 来写所有代码。

所以我做了这个东西，但前 6 个月它真的不太行。不太好用，勉强能用。我大概只用它写 10% 的代码。甚至在我们最初发布 Claude Code 之后，它也不算爆款。有不少人用，但没有今天这种指数级增长。指数增长从 Opus 4 开始——2025 年 5 月。我记得非常清楚。那就是指数增长的起点，然后每次模型发布都带来新的拐点。从 Opus 4 开始，然后 4.5，4.6，现在 4.7。每次都在拐。

但本质上，我们当时在做的是一个 pre-PMF 的产品，我们知道它在 6 个月内不会有 PMF，因为我们是在为下一代模型构建。这就是整个过程中一直的想法。你知道，Anthropic 一直非常专注。我们一直关心商业、企业、安全和编码。这就是我们一直想要的构建方式。所以在某个时刻我们知道想做一个产品，但不确定具体要做什么。Claude Code 最终成了那个产品赌注。

**Lauren Reader：** 这是个难以置信的故事，尤其是它是个意外。那你之前公开说过编码已经被"解决"了。如果这是 Anthropic 最好的三个产品之一，能不能具体说说你指的是什么，还有哪些东西可能没被解决，或者会有哪些二阶问题出现？

**Boris Cherny：** 好，我先问一下在场的人。谁还在 100% 手写代码？谁 100% 用像 Claude Code 这样的 agent 写代码？好的。谁在两者之间？好，大概 50% 解决了。

[笑声]

对我来说是 100%。Claude Code 的代码库——你们知道的，它泄露了，所以大家都知道了——它挺简单的。就是 TypeScript 和 React。没什么大秘密，没什么真正复杂的。我们选择 TypeScript 和 React 是因为它们在模型的训练分布里占比很高。当我们开始构建代码库时，模型没有今天这么智能，所以语言和框架的选择非常重要。现在，它什么都能写，能学新语言、新框架。但当时，你得用训练分布内的东西。

正因为如此，我觉得比较早的时候我们就达到了模型写 100% 代码的阶段。对我们来说，这大概发生在去年 10 月、11 月。所以现在对我来说，模型写我 100% 的代码。我通常每天提交几十个 PR。上周有一天我做了 150 个 PR——那是记录。我就是想试试能推多远。但是对我来说，编码就是已经被解决了。

不过不是所有地方都是这样。有些非常大、非常复杂的代码库。有些奇怪的语言模型还不擅长。大家都知道，它在逐步到位。通常答案就是等下一个模型。

**Lauren Reader：** 能不能讲讲你个人的工作设置？你前几天给我们演示过，确实挺疯狂的。

**Boris Cherny：** 好，我大概 6 个月前在 Twitter 上分享过我的个人设置。有意思的是，分享的时候我没意识到别人会觉得惊讶。对我来说那就是写代码的方式。

[笑声]

而且从那以后已经变了。现在我大部分工作在手机上做。我有 Claude App，打开后左侧有个 code 标签页，我在里面跑着一堆 session。大概同时有 5 到 10 个 session。每个 session 里通常有很多 agent，所以我目前大概有几百个 agent 在跑。通常每天晚上有几千个在做更深层次的工作。

**Lauren Reader：** 多少个 session？

**Boris Cherny：** 大概 5 到 10 个。每个 session 里通常有很多 agent，所以目前大概有几百个在跑。通常每天晚上有几千个在做更深层次的工作。

管理这些有几种方式。一种是让 Claude 用一堆 sub-agent 来做工作。但我发现自己越来越多用的是 loop。就是 `/loop`——这是最酷的东西。最简单有效的东西。它的本质就是让 Claude 用 cron 安排一个未来的定时任务，而且是重复执行的。可以每分钟、每 5 分钟、每天跑一次。

现在我有几十个 loop 在跑各种东西。有一个在看护我的 PR——修 CI、自动 rebase。有一个保持 CI 健康——如果有 flaky test 就去修。还有一个每 30 分钟从 Twitter 抓取反馈然后帮我聚类。我就是随时跑着一堆这些 loop。我真的觉得 loop 就是未来。如果你还没试过，强烈强烈推荐。

我们也刚推出了 Routines，是同样的东西但是在服务端跑。所以即使你关了笔记本，它也会继续运行。

**Lauren Reader：** 好，这是你的个人设置。跟我们讲讲你觉得未来的团队会是什么样子。你怎么从你现在做的工作推演出来？怎么让团队里的每个人保持同步、理解上下文？还是说我们需要把更多的事情交给 agent？

**Boris Cherny：** 嗯，我觉得——你知道，预测真的很难，但我来这儿就是做预测的，所以我试试。我感觉事情发展的方向是会有更多的通才（generalist），比今天多得多。今天我们说通才，基本上还是指工程师——他们在写代码，但可能是产品工程师。所以"通才"可能是指一个人同时做 iOS、Web 和服务器端。那是工程领域的通才。

但我觉得我们会开始看到更多的是跨学科的通才。就是说工程师同时精通产品工程，也精通设计。或者精通产品、数据科学和工程。

我不知道，这是我们团队已经开始看到的。Claude Code 团队里很多人是跨学科的通才。团队里每个人都写代码——我们的工程经理、产品经理、设计师、数据科学家、财务、用户研究员，每一个人都在写代码。你们可能在点头，但我打赌在座各位也在看到同样的趋势。

**Lauren Reader：** [清嗓子] 我再问最后一个最喜欢的问题，然后开放给观众。我们聊了编码在怎么变化。我好奇你看到软件或软件产品的世界在怎么变化。当我们看到 AI 让写代码的成本降低 10 倍或 100 倍时，用软件生产的产品的价值会怎样？我们面临一场 SaaS 末日吗？你怎么看？你又得做预测了。

**Boris Cherny：** SaaS 末日这个问题是我最喜欢的问题了。我觉得会有两件事发生，而且我觉得这两件都不是人们一直在讨论的。

第一件——在座有人听 Acquired 播客吗？对，最好的播客。我前几天居然有机会跟他们录了一期 Unplugged，感觉像见到了偶像因为主持人真的太厉害了。他们有个"七大力量"（Seven Powers）的概念——Hamilton Helmer 写的书，讲商业中的七种护城河。我觉得因为 AI，这些护城河里有些会变得更重要，有些会变得不重要。

比如，一个变得不那么重要的是切换成本（switching costs），因为你可以用模型把东西从一个产品迁移到另一个。另一个变得不那么重要的是流程壁垒（process power），因为那些护城河靠的是工作流和流程的公司——Claude 越来越擅长搞定流程。特别是 4.7，它就是能爬山（hill climb）任何东西。你给它一个目标，告诉它迭代到完成，它就会做到。我觉得这是第一个这样的模型。所以我觉得这些会变得不那么重要，但其他护城河仍然重要——网络效应、规模经济、稀缺资源。这些不会因为 AI 而改变。

第二件事——如果你看今天的创业公司数量，或者过去 10 年的，我觉得未来 10 年颠覆一切的创业公司数量会增长 10 倍。因为现在你可以是一个很小的创业公司，构建出跟大公司一样有价值的产品，而且可以正面竞争。因为大公司必须改造业务流程、改变工作方式、重新培训所有人使用技术，他们会面临巨大的内部阻力。但在座没有人有这个问题。如果你从零开始，可以从一开始就用 AI 原生地构建。

所以我不知道。我觉得现在是最好的构建时代。是最好的创业时代。巨大的颠覆即将到来。

**Lauren Reader：** 所以我们还是有希望的。谢谢你，Boris。我想开放给观众提问。有人想问什么吗？Dan？

**观众（Dan）：** 我很好奇——你说你在产品市场契合（PMF）之前构建了 6 个月，但现在模型已经足够好了，你觉得 Claude Code 的成功有多少归功于模型，多少归功于产品决策？

**Boris Cherny：** 我觉得大概是混合的。如果你问我可能半年前，比例大概是 50/50。

**观众（Dan）：** 那两年后呢？

**Boris Cherny：** 两年后？老兄，我不知道。我们的计划周期是一周。

**观众：** 几个月吧。未来的某个时候。

[笑声]

**Boris Cherny：** 顺便说一下，我觉得之所以是 50/50——你知道，我以前做过 YC。我是一家 YC 公司的第一个员工，做过好几个创业公司。在创业公司里——尤其是在 YC——他们反复灌输的一件事就是"做人们喜爱的东西"（build something people love）。所以不管产品是什么，不管模型怎么样，你最终还是要做一个人们喜爱的东西。我觉得这就是产品为什么重要的原因——我们非常关注每个小细节，让你全天使用的体验真的很好。

我觉得随着模型变好，harness（产品外壳）会变得不那么重要。我们现在在想的是怎么让 harness 进化——怎么让 loop 成为更一等的功能？怎么让运行大量 agent 更容易？Sub-agent 是一个思路，还有更多我们在做的东西。但我觉得一年后，模型会更好地对齐。所以我们今天所有的安全机制——prompt injection 防护、命令静态验证、权限模式、human in the loop——都会变得不那么重要，因为模型会自己做对的事。

**Lauren Reader：** 谢谢。

**观众：** 把麦克风扔过去，Dan？好的。我想稍微跳出软件的范围——我觉得 Claude Code 几个月前做了一个文化层面的改变，它民主化了软件开发。你可以看到店主自己给自己写软件，甚至编程控制微控制器让门打开时灯亮起来。你觉得未来，软件开发会变成一种像微软 Office 那样的技能吗？就是每个人都会做，不只是科技行业的人？

**Boris Cherny：** 天哪，是的。是的是的。我觉得甚至比那更进一步。我觉得它会变成——我不知道——会变成像"我会发短信"这样的技能。

我读的两种书基本是科幻和科技史。在科技史里，我觉得有一件事跟现在发生的最像。就是 1400 年代欧洲的印刷术。在印刷术之前，欧洲大约只有 10% 的人识字——他们知道怎么读和写。他们通常被那些不识字的国王和领主雇佣。他们的工作就是读和写，这不是每个人都会的事。

印刷术发明后——然后又有了两三台印刷机——在第一台印刷机之后的 50 年里，欧洲出版的文献比之前一千年加起来还多。同一时期，文献的成本、一本书的成本下降了大约 100 倍。然后，花了几百年——因为学读写确实难，需要教育体系、政府，不能所有人都在种地——但在接下来几百年里，全球识字率上升到了大约 70%。

所以现在我们都能读能写，你不需要一个"读写学位"才能读写。虽然仍然有专业作家，那是一个可以从事的职业。所以我觉得即将发生的事——而且会比 50 年快得多——就是软件将被完全民主化，任何人都能做。

有很多推论。比如说你在写会计软件。写会计软件的最佳人选，我觉得可能今天就已经不是工程师了，而是一个非常好的会计师——因为他们非常了解领域，而编码是简单的部分。领域知识才是难的部分。我觉得这显然就是未来。

**观众：** Greg 说的一点是你们有点"活在未来"，因为你们能提前用到模型和 agent。Claude Code 在公开发布前是内部工具。你们在工程方面和外部世界之间的差距是一个月？三个月？六个月？这个差距是在变大还是变小？

**Boris Cherny：** 所以在内部，我们用的模型跟大家一样。对我们来说，吃自己的狗粮（dog fooding）非常非常重要。我们用的东西跟在座各位用的一样。我们用一点 Mythos 来试试，然后大量使用 Opus 4.7 来吃狗粮和写大部分代码。我觉得在模型方面没有真正的差距。基本就是 Mythos，它的某个后代版本最终会对所有人可用。

我觉得在产品方面，差距可能大得多。这跟我们改变了所有流程有关。如果你跟 Anthropic 的人聊，我们用 Claude 做一切事情。我们的 Claude 整天在互相交流——当我在写代码时，我的 Claude 在 loop 里运行，它们会通过 Slack 跟其他人的 Claude 沟通来解决未知问题。我们公司没有任何手写代码了。所有 SQL 都是模型写的。一切都是模型构建的。

所以我觉得我们领先的地方不是技术——因为我们用的技术对在座每个人都是可用的。从根本上说我们在做的是一个平台，对我们来说，开发者能用跟我们一样的东西非常重要，我们发布的每样东西都自己先用。但我觉得真正大的领先其实在组织结构和组织流程方面。这方面希望我们能在这样的场合讨论，大家都能从中学习和进化。

**Lauren Reader：** 对，我觉得这也是创业公司的优势之一。从那个起点开始容易得多。Jared？

**观众（Jared）：** 嗯，上次我们聊的时候，你提到了多 agent，当时还很早期。在之前的 Sequoia 活动上你说有些东西在管线里。现在有了 `/batch`、`/loop`、sub-team、team。能不能讲讲在模型层和 harness 层面，你们怎么注入先验（priors）？模型层面的目标函数怎么变化来让委派工作、启动 agent 的体验更好？因为有很多工作是可以并行的，可以做得更快，但我感觉我需要自己判断什么时候该并行，而不是模型自己理解它可以启动 10 个 sub-agent。

**Boris Cherny：** 在产品方面，其实就是靠 prompting。就是这样。我们调整 prompt 来帮助模型更多地并行做事。但说实话，随着模型变好，它自然就会这样做了。比如 loop 这个功能，我发现 4.7 就自己开始用了，非常酷。它会做类似这样的事——我跟它说"去拉这个数据查询"，然后它说"嘿，我注意到数据在变化。我会启动一个 loop，每 30 分钟给你报告一次。"我说"太好了，能发到 Slack 吗？"然后它用 Slack MCP 做到了。

所以我觉得随着时间推移，不应该由用户来学怎么更好地使用工具。如果是那样的话，其实是产品设计的问题，是我没做好。应该由模型来更好地做这些事，由我们来 prompting 让它自然地做到。

**观众：** 现在似乎很多人用 Claude 或 Codex 这些云端工具做大部分计算。但也有一些人很积极地倡导本地 AI。随着开源模型追赶上来，这可能会成为更多人的选择。我好奇你对未来几年的看法——你觉得大家会继续依赖云端集中式计算，还是会转向本地 agent？

**Boris Cherny：** 我觉得——可能有几种方式来回答。最根本的回答是：这不重要。因为我觉得我们快到了模型自己能搞定这些的阶段。我觉得再过几年，模型会自己写所有代码、启动 agent、构建环境。如果它觉得用本地模型更好，那它就会用本地模型。这些不再是我们作为工程师要做的决定了。

**Lauren Reader：** 我们还有时间再问几个问题。Jamie？

**观众（Jamie）：** 谢谢。感觉 Claude Code 的一个很好的决策是利用了很多开发者的工具和工作流是本地的这个事实。但对于一般的知识工作——用云端工具的——不一定是这样。我好奇你怎么想 Co-work 的事——怎么给 Co-work 足够的工具访问权限，让它像 Claude Code 对开发者一样强大？

**Boris Cherny：** 这是个很好的问题。我在大公司的时候，我们花了大概 5 年把所有环境迁移到远程，大规模的时候这工作量巨大。但对知识工作来说，大部分已经在那了——Salesforce、Docs 之类的。对我们来说，答案就是最简单的：MCP。你在 Claude AI 里有的那个 MCP 连接器，你接上 Salesforce、Google Docs、Google Calendar，然后 Co-work 就能用了，Claude CLI 也能用，Claude Code 到处都能用。

**Lauren Reader：** 那对于没有 MCP 的系统呢——你觉得 computer use 会是个大机会吗？

**Boris Cherny：** 对，我觉得 computer use 算是个兜底方案。据我所知，Anthropic 在 computer use 方面相当领先。如果你通过 Co-work 使用它，效果相当好。它能操作你电脑上几乎任何软件。很慢，但做得很好，特别是 4.7。但除此之外，MCP 就是答案。所有这些其实都不太重要——可以是 MCP、API、某种程序化访问——因为模型不在乎，对模型来说就是 token。

**Lauren Reader：** 好，最后一个问题。Ryan？

**观众（Ryan）：** 谢谢。你之前提到过这一点——如果之前你看到了产品溢出并决定做一个会随着模型变好而变得更有趣的产品，你能不能说说（哪怕模糊地），你今天会做什么样的产品——一个在 6 个月到 1 年后随着模型进步会变得更有趣的产品？

**Boris Cherny：** 嗯，Claude Design 我觉得是一个很好的例子。现在已经挺好了，会变得更好。还有一些我们正在为 Claude Code 开发的东西，会在未来几周上线，你们会看到的。然后我觉得 loop 和 batch 这些围绕大规模并行 agent 的东西会变得更好。Computer use 也是另一个好方向。

**Lauren Reader：** 好，Boris，非常感谢你的加入。如果有人还有问题我们会多待一会儿。

[掌声]

**Boris Cherny：** 谢谢大家。

original_en.body_md:
**Moderator (Asia):** Okay, I'm excited to introduce our next speaker. Show of hands, who here uses Claude code? Okay, show of hands, who here has Claude code psychosis? Come on guys, [clears throat] it's okay. It's okay. Um my my my team lovingly says I have Claude code psychosis, which may or may not be true. Um we are delighted to have Boris Cherny with us today. Uh Boris is the creator, the father of Claude code. Um and uh in the process of doing that has just had a front row seat to to reinventing uh the modern way of of software development. Um and we're we're really grateful to you, Boris, for taking the time to speak with us today. We know that um the entirety of software development kind of rests on your shoulders. So, thank you for taking it out of your time to be with us today. And interviewing Boris is Lauren Reader from our team. Thank you.

[applause]

**Lauren Reader:** Giving our chairs. Um you took my you took my opening line, Asia. We asked who here uses Claude code. There's a lot of hands. That's awesome. Thank you for joining us, Boris. It's very special to have you here. Um as a roomful of builders, I think you are changing building entirely. And so, I'm very curious to explore how you think about the future of software, coding, and what we should spend all of our free time on. Um but I'll give you a me a tiny bit more background on you so that everyone has a little bit more context. So, beyond creating Claude code, Boris is very much an engineer's engineer. You were writing a lot of code through your whole career, writing textbooks about code, including programming in TypeScript. Um and I think last time we chatted you hadn't written a single line of code in the last year, or at least so far in 2026, which is quite the change.

**Boris Cherny:** Um There's also a a little known thing back in middle school, I wrote a guide about uh writing BASIC for TI-83 Plus calculators. And I I just I I searched for it, it's actually still on the internet. It's extremely embarrassing, so please don't search it. But it exists.

[laughter]

**Lauren Reader:** We will definitely be finding that. Um so, we're going to do I'm going to start with a few questions here. Maybe we'll start with a little bit of the history of Claude code, how you started it, and then we're going to have a lot of audience Q&A for this one. And so, start thinking about your questions in the back of your head, uh and would love to turn it over to you all soon. Yeah. Um and also real quick, so for people that use Claude code, do people use the CLI mostly? Like okay, majority CLI? Okay. That's a lot. Majority desktop? Okay. Majority VS code or JetBrains IDE? Okay. That's actually not a lot. Okay. Other?

**Boris Cherny:** I'm like iOS mostly these days. Yeah.

[laughter]

Okay. Cool. Um yeah, so I started Claude code kind of accidentally in a in a lot of ways. Um I joined this team back in late 2024. It was a sort of this incubator within Anthropic called Anthropic Labs. And uh the team kind of served its purpose. Um we created Claude code, uh MCP, and the desktop app. It was a team it was just a few of us. So, very much like innovation team. We built the thing that we wanted to build, we disbanded the team. Uh now the team's actually back together for round two. Mike Krieger, who's the you know, like the chief product officer at at Anthropic and used to be one of the founders at Instagram, so he's leading that right now.

Um so the kind of the the the the reason that I started to work on coding is we felt like there was this product overhang. And I I'm guessing people here use that word a lot. Uh but we definitely use this word a lot in kind of within the lab. Uh there's this idea that the model can do all the stuff that no product has yet captured. And in late 2024, when we were looking at coding, the way that we did coding, the state of the art at the time was type ahead. It was you open your IDE and you press tab and you can like complete like one line at a time. And that was the thing that Sonnet 3.5 enabled for the first time. But the feeling was we could actually go a lot further than that. And the model was almost ready for the next big step. So, we don't have to do type ahead anymore, we can just have the agent write all of the code.

And so, I built it, and it just really didn't work for the first 6 months. It was like not very good. It was barely usable. I wrote it from I used it for maybe 10% of my code or something like that. And even after we released Claude code initially, it was not a hit. There's a lot of people that used it, but it did not have this exponential growth that it has today. Um that started with Opus 4 in May. And I I remember that very clearly. That's like when the exponential growth started, and then it kind of inflected with every model release. Uh like it started with Opus 4, then 4.5, then 4.6, now 4.7. It just kind of keeps inflecting.

But essentially, we were trying to build this thing that was like pre-PMF, and we knew that it wouldn't have PMF for 6 months because we were building for the next model. And that was the idea the pretty much the whole time. And you know, for Anthropic in general, we've always just been very focused. We've always cared about business and enterprise and safety and coding. That's just always been kind of the way that we wanted to build. And so, at some point we kind of knew that we wanted to build a product. We didn't know exactly what we wanted. So, this kind of ended up being the the product bet.

**Lauren Reader:** It's an incredible story, especially that it was an accident. Um so, you've said on the record that you think coding is solved. Uh if this is one of the three best from Anthropic, can you tell us more about what you mean by that, and what might still not be solved, or what second-order problems might come?

**Boris Cherny:** All right. I can ask another question for the room. Who writes 100% of their code by hand? Who writes 100% of their code using a agent like Claude code? Okay. Who's like somewhere in between? Okay. So, like 50% solved.

[laughter]

I mean, for me it's for me it's like for me it's 100%. Like the the Claude code code base, um you know, it leaked, so you know, people know. Uh it's pretty simple. It's just like TypeScript and it's React. Like there's no big secret. There's there's nothing really complicated. The the reason we picked TypeScript and React is it's very on distribution for the model. So, when we started, you know, building the code base, the model was not as intelligent as it is today, so the language and the framework mattered a lot. Nowadays, you know, it can write whatever, and it can pick up new languages, new frameworks it hasn't seen. But back then, you wanted to use something pretty on distribution.

Because of that, I think fairly early we got to the point where the model just wrote 100% of the code. And for us, this happened sometime in October, November last year. And so, for me today, you know, like the model writes 100% of my code. I write somewhere, you know, usually a few dozen PRs every day. Uh there was a day last week I did like 150 PRs in a day. That was like that was a record. I was just trying to kind of push to see how far I can get it. Um but yeah, it's like for me for me it's just solved.

Um but this is not the case everywhere. There's very big complicated code bases. There's kind of weird languages the model's not good at yet. Um and you know, as everyone here knows, it's it's getting there. Usually the answer is just wait for the next model.

**Lauren Reader:** Can you actually tell us about your personal setup? You walked us through it the other day. It is pretty wild.

**Boris Cherny:** Yeah. Um so, I shared my personal setup like 6 months ago or something on on Twitter. And it it's funny, I actually I shared it I didn't realize that it would be surprising for anyone. That was just like the way that I coded.

[laughter]

And it's changed since then. It's changed. Um and so, now actually most of my work I do from my phone. Um and so, I don't know if like you guys won't be able to see this, but I have um so, I have like the Claude app, and if you open the Claude app, on the left-hand side, there's this little code tab, and I just have a bunch of sessions going. Um you you probably can't see it.

**Lauren Reader:** How many sessions?

**Boris Cherny:** Uh usually have like maybe like five to 10 sessions. Uh and then the sessions usually have a bunch of agents, so I think currently probably like a few hundred agents going. Um usually every night I have like a few thousand that are doing kind of deeper work.

There's a few ways to manage it. One is that you ask Claude to use a bunch of sub-agents to do work. Actually, the the thing that I've been finding myself using more and more is the loop. So, this is /loop, and it's just like the coolest thing. It's like the simplest thing that works. All it is is you have Claude use cron to schedule a job for some point in the future, and it's a repeat job. And it can run every every minute, every 5 minutes, every day, kind of however often you want to schedule it.

And at this point, I have like dozens of loops that are running for stuff. So, I have one that's babysitting my PRs, like fixing CI, auto-rebasing. I have another one that keeps CI healthy. So, like if there's like a flaky test or whatever, it'll it'll go and fix it. Um I have another one that grabs uh feedback from Twitter and kind of clusters it for me every 30 minutes. So, I just have a bunch of these loops running at any time. I sort of feel like loops are the future at this point. If you haven't experimented with it, highly highly recommend it.

And we also just launched routines, which is the same thing but kind of on the server. So, even if you close your laptop, it it keeps going.

**Lauren Reader:** So, that's your personal setup. Tell us about what you think teams will look like in the future. How do you extrapolate from all the work you're doing to keep everyone on the team moving forward, understanding the context, or do you think we need to let go of a lot more to agents to make it work?

**Boris Cherny:** Um I think so I you know, it's like it's so hard to make predictions, but um I'm here to make predictions, so I'll try to make some. I I I feel like the way that things are going is generally there's going to be a lot more generalists than there are today. And today when we talk about generalists, I think largely we're talking about people that are still engineers. So, they're still writing code, but maybe they're kind of product engineers. So, maybe when we say generalist, it's like a you know, they do iOS and web and server, for example. That's like a generalist in engineering.

But I think the thing that we're going to start to see a lot more of is generalists that are cross-disciplinary. So, this is engineers that are really good at product engineering, but also really great at design. Or really great at product and data science and engineering.

Um I don't know. It's it's something that we're starting to see on our team. So, actually like a lot of people on the Claude code team are generalists across disciplines. Everyone on our team codes. So, like our engineering manager, our product manager, our designers, our data scientist, our finance guy, our user researcher, every single person on our team writes code. And so, you know, like they're specialist in something, but now also everyone's just coding. And you know, I'm seeing some nods, but I bet also it's actually not that surprising to people in this room cuz I bet you're seeing the same things.

**Lauren Reader:** Um [clears throat] I'll have one more favorite questions then we'll open up to the audience. So, we talked a bit about what's changing with coding. I'm curious about what you see changing in the world of software or software products. Um I think as we see AI making writing code 10 or 100x cheaper, what happens to the value of the products that are produced with software? Do we have a SAS apocalypse on our hands? How do you think this plays out? And again, you're going to have to make another prediction.

**Boris Cherny:** The SAS apocalypse question is my favorite question then. Um I think there's two things that are going to happen and I I don't think either of them is the thing that people have been talking about.

I think one is Is anyone here an acquired listener? Like the acquired podcast? Yeah, it's like the best podcast. Uh I actually I I got to do a unplugged with them the other week and I I just I I felt like I got to like meet my heroes cuz they're they're just like the hosts are the best. So, they have this idea of uh seven powers and and this is a this is like Hamilton. He kind of wrote he wrote a book about this and this is kind of the seven modes in business. And I think what's going to happen is because of AI, some of these modes are going to get more important and some are going to get less important.

And so, like for example, one that gets less important is uh switching costs because you can just use the model and you can kind of port from one thing to a different thing. Another one that gets less important is process power because for companies whose mode is like workflows and process and things like this, Claude is getting really good at figuring out process. And especially with 4.7, it can just hill climb anything. So, if you give it a target and you tell it to iterate until it's done, it will just do it. I think this is the first model like that. So, I think these are going to get less important, but I think the previous modes actually still matter. So, this is like network effects, uh scale economies, cornered resources, things like that. These are not really changing with AI.

I think the second thing is if you look at the number of startups today or like maybe in the next you know, the past 10 years, I think the number of startups in the next 10 years that are just going to like disrupt everything is going to increase like 10x. Because right now you can be a tiny startup, you could build a thing that's as valuable as a large company and you can actually compete head-to-head because the large company has to evolve their business process, they have to evolve the way they work, they have to retrain everyone to use technology, they're going to face a lot of internal resistance to that. But you know, no one here has that problem. If you're starting fresh, then you can kind of build with AI natively from the ground up.

So, I don't know. I I think it's the best time to build. It's the best time to be a startup. It's there's so much disruption coming.

I I think in tech history, there's one thing which I think to me is the clearest parallel for what's happening right now. And this is in the 1400s, the printing press in Europe. And what what happened was before the printing press, essentially 10% of the European population was literate. They knew how to read and write. They were often employed by like kings and lords that were not literate. And their job was to you know, their their job was to read and write and this is not something that everyone knew how to do.

The printing press was invented, then there were two more presses and in the 50 years after the first printing press, there was more literature published in Europe than in the thousand years before. And over the same period, the cost of literature, the cost of a book went down like a 100x. And then, you know, it took a couple hundred years cuz you know, learning to read and write is hard. You need education systems and government and everyone can't be working on farms and so on. But over the next few hundred years, literacy globally went up to like 70%.

And so, you know, now we can all read and write and you don't need a a degree in reading and writing to know how to read and write. So, I I think the thing that's about to happen and it's going to be much faster than 50 years is software will be a thing that is fully democratized, that anyone can do.

I think on the product side, there's probably a far larger gap. And that's just related to us changing all of our processes. Like if you talk to people at Anthropic, we use Claude for literally everything. And our Claudes are talking all day like as as I'm coding, as my Claudes are coding in a loop, they will communicate over Slack to talk to other people's Claudes that are also running in a loop to kind of figure out unknowns. We have no more manually written code anywhere at the company. All of the SQL is written by uh by models. Everything is just built by the models.

So, I I I think actually the place that we're ahead is not the technology cuz the same technology available to us is available to everyone here because fundamentally, we are building a platform. And so, for us, it's really important that developers can use the same thing that we're using and that we we dog food everything that we put out there. But I think there's actually a far bigger weed in kind of the organizational structure and organizational process.
